article_title: Celebrate Eater’s New Cookbook With Us at a Stylish NYC Bar
author: Missy Frederick
article_summary: Walking tacos recipe featured in the “Eaterland” cookbook. | Matt Taylor-Gross Eater is debuting its second cookbook, Eaterland: Recipes and Stories From Across the United States, at the end of this month, and you’re invited. The event is scheduled for Tuesday, April 28, at 6 p.m. at the stylish Armada on the Lower East Side. The event will feature recipes from Eaterland. Join us and raise a glass with the cookbook’s editors and members of Eater’s staff. One $45 ticket includes two drinks, light bites from the recipes featured in the book, and a copy of the cookbook. Get your tickets here. Ticket prices increase to $55 at the door, so grab those tickets now. With a focus on the iconic regional dishes that define American cuisine, Eaterland is full of recipes, observations from chefs, and essays about unique local ingredients and traditions. Every chapter features recipes for dishes crucial to the region’s history, from the french fry-topped Pittsburgh salad to Hawaii’s loco moco — many from small restaurants integral to the local community. If you can’t make it to the event, you can still preorder the cookbook here. Use code EATERBOOKS40 for 40% off.

article_title: An Italian Institution Is Reborn — Plus, More NYC Restaurant Openings to Know in April
author: Tierney Plumb
article_summary: Bar Ferdinando takes over the treasured Brooklyn home of the former Ferdinando’s Focacceria. | Scott Semler/Eater This is Eater’s guide to all the new restaurants, bars, and cafes that have opened this week in April 2026. Throughout the month, we’ll update the list weekly (see: March, February, January). When we’ve been to a place, we will then include a few openings on our heatmaps (Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, cocktail bars) to let you know the ones we like. If there’s an opening in your neighborhood that we’ve missed, let us know at ny@eater.com. April 15 Spotlight Carroll Gardens: It’s opening day (Wednesday, April 15) for Bar Ferdinando, a new Italian bar and cafe that revives the 121-year-old home of Ferdinando’s Focacceria. The all-day replacement comes from Sal Lamboglia, the Brooklyn restaurateur behind Cafe Spaghetti, Swoony’s, and Sal Tang’s, who was tapped by Ferdinando’s Focacceria owner Francesco “Frank” Buffa to take over after he shuttered the institution last year. The daytime menu revives classics like Sicilian arancini, octopus salad, sandwiches on house-made focaccia, and the famed pane e panelle (chickpea fritters) using Ferdinando’s family recipe, plus desserts like bomboloni and olive oil cakes. The bar spotlights amari-based drinks, martinis, vermouth, and a take on the Manhattan Special, the iconic Brooklyn-based espresso soda. 151 Union Street, at Hicks Street, Brooklyn And the rest… Astoria: Elevator technician-turned-sandwich maker Eddie Sosa debuted a new ghost kitchen this month called Sosa Sandwich Shop. “My sandwiches are the stories of my life, from growing up in my grandma’s kitchen in Queens to fine dining restaurants throughout NYC,” he tells Eater. Along with Cuban sandwiches and arepas, monthly sandwich specials are inspired by stories of his own social media followers. 24-26 47th Street, at Brooklyn Queens Expressway Boerum Hill: Sea & Soil, a worker-owned sandwich shop and bakery, opened on Tuesday, April 14 after closing its previous location in Carroll Gardens in April 2025. The sandwiches here are available with sliding-scale pricing as part of the shop’s mission to make “simple, thoughtful, and healthy food that is accessible to all people while keeping profits in the hands of those who do the labor and the communities in which the food is made.” Espresso drinks will be available at a later date. The shop is also working on getting a beer and wine license. 388 Atlantic Avenue, between Bond and Hoyt streets Bushwick: All-halal Italian restaurant Thick & Thin Pizza recently debuted in Brooklyn with New York- and Detroit-style pies on sourdough served whole or by the slice, notes Infatuation. The European-style bistro also serves pizza sandwiches, pastas, lasagna, meatballs, and tiramisu. 1302 Myrtle Avenue, at Stockholm Street  Chelsea: Moonburger, the burger chain that started in the Hudson Valley in 2021, opened its newest location on Thursday, April 9 inside Olly Olly Market. It’s the third location in the city. Although the burger shop started with a vegetarian focus, it has since adopted meat from small farms in New York, writing on its website that “more people identify as meat eaters” than when it first opened. It joins vendors like Forsyth Fire Escape and the new Nikkei-ish in the food hall. 601 West 26th Street, near 11th Avenue East Village: Late-night slider joint Whit’s joined the neighborhood on Thursday, April 9, keeping customers fed well into the night with onion rings, fries, deep-fried ripper hot dogs, and burgers built with Pat Lafrieda patties. Sliders topped with sliced onions cooked on the griddle start at just $3.50. 34 St Marks Place, at Second Avenue Elmhurst: GangJong Thunpa Phunshi Restaurant opened on Wednesday, April 8, augmenting a diverse dining stretch of Queens known as “International Mile” with an array of Tibetan street foods. Look for momo dumplings, cold laphing, mapo tofu, hand-pulled noodle soups, butter tea, and other Himalayan favorites in the narrow, orange space marked by an elephant logo. 81-20 Broadway, between 81st and 82nd streets, Queens Financial District: Modern Greek restaurant Lithos brought big Mykonos energy to the heart of FiDi on Wednesday, April 1. Surrounded in curved white stone and a warm interior glow, Lithos sends out skillets of seafood-studded orzo, grilled lamb chops marinated in Greek herbs, whole Mediterranean sea bass, and vibrant cocktails from its back-lit onyx bar. 151 Maiden Lane, at Front Street Financial District: Situated on the 20th floor of the Club Quarters Hotel, scenic rooftop bar Golden Hour at One40 comes to life on Wednesday, April 15. Part of the previously opened One40 Rooftop – the woman-owned, modern American restaurant from Mona Panjwani – the new outdoor extension features martini-and-caviar service, deviled eggs dressed with Iberico ham, and fried oysters. As the name suggests, the rooftop respite embraces New York’s golden hour at sunset with natural textures, lush greenery, and comfy lounge seating featuring panoramic views of Lower Manhattan and the Hudson River. One40 Rooftop just got a spring menu makeover, featuring fresh vegetables and seafood, led by Paris native Sébastien Chaoui. 140 Washington Street, between Albany and Cedar streets Greenpoint: Arthur opened on Friday, April 10 with $36 steak and brioche martinis, in the space previously occupied by Fulgurances Laundromat, which closed at the end of last year. Chef Kevin Finch, an alum of San Francisco’s three-Michelin-starred Atelier Crenn, and his wife Alexa, are running the restaurant. Kevin Finch previously had residencies at the Fulgurances locations in both Paris and Brooklyn. There is a lot of new energy on this block of Franklin Street, with Gigi’s (from the Fulgurances team) and Sonny’s Corner also having opened recently. 132 Franklin Street, between Milton Street and Greenpoint Avenue Greenpoint: Greenpoint Comedy Club, an entertainment venue with an ambitious beverage program, opened on Friday, April 10. The speakeasy spotlights local beers, biodynamic wines, vermouth, sake, nonalcoholic options, house-made infusions, and proprietary mixes. Open from afternoons until 2 a.m., the 2,000-square-foot venue equipped with high-end lighting and A/V capabilities comes from Jeremy Pinsly – a longtime stand-up comedian in NYC – and his wife, Kayla Pinsly. In addition to hosting comedy acts from both rising and established talent, look for live music, movie screenings, burlesque nights, comedy album recordings, and even after-school programs. 66 Greenpoint Avenue, between Franklin and West streets Greenwich Village: Following its Flatiron debut last fall, Miami-born Mediterranean restaurant Motek opened its second NYC location on Tuesday, April 14, with $5 happy hour deals, a prime corner patio with room for 50, and a 120-seat dining room. A yellow-tiled bar up front sets the sunny seaside tone, along with real flowers, umbrella-shaped light fixtures, marble tabletops, white banquettes, and arched walkways. An open kitchen in the back sends out Motek favorites like laffa, shawarma, schnitzel, and meze. 184 Bleecker Street, at MacDougal Street Lower East Side: After Eden, a Vietnamese coffee shop by day and bar by night, opened on Friday, April 10. In the morning, expect drinks like jackfruit lattes and cà phê muối (salted coffee with condensed milk and heavy cream), all made with Vietnamese coffee beans. The night menu will bring cocktails like the pho-tini, with sotol and Scotch steeped in a pho-inspired vermouth reduction. Industry veteran Gelo Honrade is behind the project. The food menu will include bánh mì and pastries like kouign amann with fish-sauce caramel. 162 Orchard Street, between Stanton and Rivington streets Long Island City: Greenpoint’s Stuart Cinema & Cafe opened its new location to private events on Friday, April 10, with its public grand opening set for April 24. The independently owned theater opened in 2018 and operated in Greenpoint until last month, with owner Emelyn Stuart citing recent problems with the cinema’s landlord. Last fall, Greenpointers reported that the landlord of the building complex housing the cinema had sent some of its commercial tenants last-minute lease terminations. 37-18 Northern Boulevard, near 39th Street Midtown: A contemporary Latin American steakhouse called Amasa opened inside the Kimpton Era Hotel on Tuesday, April 14, bringing huacatay herb-marinated bavette steak, Peruvian roasted chicken, colossal shrimp, fiery salsas, and agave cocktails, next to Rockefeller Center. The hotel’s anchor restaurant, led by chef Christopher Loeffl, opens in tandem with Bar Amasa, its casual sibling centered around fresh guacamole, regional tacos, and margs.  32 W. 48th Street, between Fifth and Sixth avenues Noho: Go Greek, the Southern California yogurt chain that started in 2012, opened its first location in NYC on Sunday, April 12 with classic yogurt, by-weight frozen yogurt, smoothies, and more. The brand has ambitious expansion plans in the coming years, with a goal of 50 locations globally by 2027. Its opening adds more fuel to the city’s recently revitalized frozen yogurt scene, along with recent openings like Mimi’s and Birdie’s. 683 Broadway, at West Third Street Upper East Side: Throwback hangout Wainwright’s Tavern opened on Friday, April 10, with a classic American menu full of oysters, pastas, salads, cucumber martinis, and a burger that’s already drawing comparisons to Minetta Tavern. (With slick maroon booths, wooden walls, checkered flooring, and vintage decor, Wainwright’s sure looks like it, too.) “I hope it becomes a neighborhood watering hole,” writes its namesake owner Jay Wainwright, who ran now-closed seafood chain Seamore’s. 1278 Third Avenue, near E. 73rd Street West Village: Yesterday’s, a neighborhood spot for wood-fired pizzas, classic cocktails, game days, throwback music, and everything in between, debuted on Friday, April 10. Old Fashioneds, martinis, margaritas, espresso martinis, French 75s, Negronis, and mojitos join 15 beers, seltzers, wines, and lots of nonalcoholic options. Yesterday’s, which has partnership ties to East Village bar Waiting on a Friend, takes tips from “old-school New York bar culture” with wood finishes, banquette seating, nods to popular cinematic scenes, disco balls, and TVs turned on with discretion. The 6,500-square-foot, two-level newcomer anchored by a big bar rolls out brunch this spring. 157 Bleecker Street, near Thompson Street April 8 Spotlight Greenpoint: The team behind Brooklyn’s recently shuttered Fulgurances Laundromat, a chef residency restaurant born in Paris, opened pink neon-lit Gigi’s, on Wednesday, April 8. The restaurant comes from Fulgurances co-founder Hugo Hivernat and the wine team’s Thibault Dubreuil and Pierre Buffet. They’ve busted out their stockpiled inventory of European and French bottles to pair with its trending rotisserie chicken menu served with a trio of sauces. In addition to roasting mains beyond poultry (cauliflower, duck, and pork), chef Thomas Knodell’s straightforward, a la carte lineup includes a frisée salad, local cheeses from Tommy’s Coltsfoot Farm, charcuterie, terrines, chicken consomme, chicken fat-roasted potatoes, and more with help from Émile Desme, the head chef of Paris restaurant Le Chateaubriand. Gigi’s will revive the culinary incubator program that four-year-old Fulgurances was known for. 138 Franklin Street, near Greenpoint Avenue  And the rest… Long Island City: Dumpling Wang (蒸旺小笼), a restaurant serving Shanghai cuisine, opened on Friday, April 3. Its signature dishes include xiao long bao, sesame-oil pork-kidney noodles, and Shanghai-style wontons with shepherd’s purse greens. For its grand opening celebrations, the restaurant will offer 12 percent off entire orders from April 13 to April 15. The restaurant will be open daily from 7 a.m. to 1 a.m. 42-37 27th Street, at 42nd Road Lower East Side: Ms. Alice, a new cocktail bar from the team behind Hole In The Wall, opened on April 3. The bar, which bills itself as being inspired by European aperitivo culture, will offer “apero hour” every weekday from 4 p.m. to 6 p.m., with $12 negronis, spritzes, martinis, and more. Its regular cocktail menu will offer creative riffs on classic drinks, including a gibson martini made with a pickled blueberry and a red pepper paloma. The food will focus on Italian bar snacks, like gnocco fritto topped with prosciutto and stracciatella. 79 Rivington Street, at Allen Street Rockaway Beach: Tracy Obolsky’s Rockaway Beach Bakery opened its new location on Friday, April 3, inside the Rockaway Hotel, serving the same croissants, sandwiches, and pies that locals and visiting beachgoers have come to know and love over the past decade. Obolsky announced the closure of the bakery’s previous location in February, teasing “big things coming.” In May, the bakery will expand its hours to seven days a week. 108-10 Rockaway Beach Drive, between Beach 108th Street and Beach 109th Street Sunnyside: The Screen Door, the seasonal ice cream shop that got its start in Greenpoint, opened its second location on Friday, April 3. Aside from scoops, the shop serves sundaes, “flurry sundaes” (soft serve with mix-ins), milkshakes, and other frozen treats, including “ice cream nachos” made with pieces of waffle cones drizzled with hot fudge. Both shops are now open for the season. 48-19 Skillman Avenue, at 49th Street Nomad: Golden Child, a rooftop bar, opened at the Hotel Park Ave NYC on Tuesday, April 7. Keeping in theme with the bar’s name, the cocktail menu will be broken into sections like “Class Project,” referring to shareable drinks such as a punch made with mezcal, tequila, Aperol, pineapple, and passionfruit. The food menu will be anchored by the signature “Golden Child Lunch Box,” a metal tin packed with a baguette sandwich, a chocolate chip cookie, and a boozy juice pouch. The restaurant Park Rose and the cocktail bar Seed Library also opened in the hotel recently. 444 Park Avenue South, at East 30th Street Carroll Gardens: The team behind Brooklyn seafood spot Greenpoint Fish & Lobster took over recently closed Bar Bruno, reopening after three months on Monday, April 6, under the same name with a different menu. Greenpoint Fish & Lobster’s chef Orion Russell tells Eater his new Oaxacan dishes include a half-chicken with mole; more seafood like whole broiled fish; enchiladas, tacos, and burritos. It appears that Brooklyn’s other recently shuttered Bar Bruno in Greenpoint – situated at the storied corner that housed popular dive Enid’s until 2019 – will also get a new life under Greenpoint Fish & Lobster’s watch, per liquor license filings flagged by Greenpointers listing a co-owner’s name, though the concept is still TBA. 520 Henry Street, at Union Street, Brooklyn Greenpoint: The team behind Williamsburg restaurant Cozy Royale, expanded in Brooklyn on Saturday, April 4, with a cozy corner destination for frothy Guinness pours and loaded hot dogs in the old Fin Du Monde space. At the Peek Inn, Brent Young of the Meat Hook and business partner Mike Haigis capture the spirit of Pittsburgh and New England establishments near and dear to their heart with a no-frills hangout marked by one tiny TV and retro beer ads. Billed as neither a dive nor cocktail bar, the pint-sized Peek Inn spotlights $10 deals during the week (4 p.m. to 7 p.m.) like a double-dog feature, martinis, wines, or ham sliders, plus a beer-and-shot combo for just $5. 38 Driggs Avenue at Sutton Street   Hell’s Kitchen: Modern American restaurant and globe-trotting wine bar Hudson Local debuted on Tuesday, April 7. Executive chef Carlos Letona, an alum of Michelin-starred kitchens Per Se and Jean-Georges, sends out smoked mushroom croquettes, pork belly bao, and miso-glazed Chilean sea bass to start. Sommelier Elia Chuaqui’s 21 by-the-glass wine list includes skin-contact, sparkling, and full-bodied reds. During weekday happy hour, six ($16) wine flights starring Burgundy are each named for a Broadway character – like Rizzo and Evita – as a nod to the neighborhood. Letona also runs the kitchen at rooftop sibling Hudson VU, where Hudson Local’s wine club members can cut the line to soak up the city’s skyline. 653 Eleventh Avenue, at West 44th Street Williamsburg: Cocktail bar Echo Lake swings open on Wednesday, April 8, with $12 daily daiquiris and a lager/rum shot combo cutely called Tiny and a Tot, plus seafood-centric plates like oysters with tepache mignonette and green coconut curry chowder, in the former original Gertie space. Echo Lake comes from beverage power-couple Chloe Frechette (the former executive editor at Eater sibling site Punch) and Paul McGee, who cut his teeth at Chicago’s now-closed tropical bar Lost Lake. 357 Grand Street, at Marcy Avenue, Brooklyn   April 1 Spotlight opening Soho: Executive chef and owner Hirohisa Hayashi turned his Japanese restaurant Hirohisa into a new soba spot last month. Soba Ulala fully opened on Tuesday, March 31, focusing on a la carte house-made soba with hot or cold broths and toppings like duck breast or vegetables. Per the press release, the noodles are made twice a day using a mixture of buckwheat flour from upstate New York and a premium wheat flour from Japan. The rest of the menu includes small dishes like sashimi, tamago, fish collars, rice, and dessert, alongside lunchtime set meals and rice bowls. For drinks, there are sakes, shochu, beers, and wines from Japan, the U.S., and Europe. Hayashi plans on moving Hirohisa to a new location in the same neighborhood, to be announced later. 73 Thompson Street, near Spring Street And the rest… Financial District: Cafe Fleuri, the floral-filled replacement to Vienna-themed Schilling, already has some drama attached to it since opening in late March; per the New York Times, its executive chef, Adam Baumgart, has already stepped down. Chef-owner Edi Frauneder’s menu spotlights Southern France with touches of North Africa, notes the newspaper, with opening dishes like poached haddock with clams and white bean barigoule; braised lamb shoulder with merguez and turnips; and rice pudding with rhubarb and pistachios. 109 Washington Street, at Carlisle Street Financial District: Golden Mall’s new location in Manhattan quietly opened on Monday, March 30, with a first set of vendors that includes bubble tea chain Xing Fu Tang, a new location of Zhengxin Chicken Steak, and amusement center Max Fun Land. The long-awaited space is expected to eventually feature even more retail and restaurants. Golden Mall opened its first location in Flushing in 1991, and it recently received a major renovation, reopening in 2024. 47 Broadway, between Morris and Rector streets Hell’s Kitchen: Tachi, a 12-seat omakase counter with a two-room sake bar for walk-ins only, opened on Thursday, March 26. Named for the tachi blade on the Japanese sword, the minimalist space designed by BA+D features polished marble, razor-thin metals, hinoki wood, and a lengthy mural painted by hand. A la carte sushi is available, too. 339 West 44th Street, near Ninth Avenue Lower East Side: Cocktail Omakase opened on Friday, March 27, from the group behind hit cocktail bars Superbueno and Katana Kitten. The small bar will offer, as the name suggests, a Tokyo-inspired cocktail tasting menu featuring four cocktails and four paired bites, with a menu that will change every two weeks. Seatings are 60 minutes and run $55 per person, but walk-ins are welcome at the adjoining Bar 7, which will serve a la carte drinks and food. 217 Eldridge Street, near Stanton Street Midtown: Jollibee, the Filipino fast-food favorite for fried chicken and burgers, opened its third Manhattan restaurant on Tuesday, March 31. 14 East 42nd Street, near Grand Central Station Midtown East: Philippe Chow opened its glitzy new Fifth Avenue flagship on Friday, March 27. After more than two decades on the Upper East Side, the celebrity hot spot relocates to “Luxury Row” with familiar favorites from chef Philippe Chow like whole Beijing duck carved tableside, chicken satay (with a Moët Champagne tasting option), lemon-and-pepper lobster, dumplings, and cotton candy baked Alaska. The two-story space is substantially bigger than the original, with multiple private dining spaces and a striking 20-seat bar slinging its ever-popular lychee martinis. 10 East 52nd Street, at Fifth Avenue Multiple locations: Georgian restaurant Chama Mama opened two new bakeries in mid-March, one next to its original location in Chelsea and the other next to its new Bushwick location. Open 8 a.m. to 6 p.m., Let’s Chama! seats 14 and serves khachapuri, mushroom pie, chocolate cigaretti, and more. 149 West 14th Street, at Seventh Avenue; 81 Morgan Avenue, at Ingraham Street Prospect Heights: Pies ‘n’ Thighs expanded with a second location on Wednesday, April 1. The new outpost brings a more family-friendly approach, with the addition of dishes like chicken tenders and other new menu options, owner Sarah Sanneh told Eater NY. This year marks the restaurant’s 20th anniversary; its Williamsburg location has been in the same space since 2010. 244 Flatbush Avenue, at St. Mark’s Avenue Soho: Dean’s, the highly anticipated British pub from Jess Shadbolt and Annie Shi of King, opened on March 31, right next door to King. Influenced by Shadbolt’s coastal hometown of Aldeburgh in England, Dean’s emphasizes seafood; stargazy pie, in which a fish head pokes out of the crust, anchors the menu. Naturally, there will also be beer-battered fish and chips. Most of the seats will be for walk-ins to retain a casual, no-frills feel. 213 Sixth Avenue, near King Street Union Square: Guardian at the W — a new rooftop bar billed as the first in Union Square — opened atop the reinvented hotel on Monday, March 30,  just in time for spring. The scenic perch located 22 stories up caps off the  $100-million renovation of the W with cocktails, small bites, and views of the downtown skyline from restaurateur John McDonald (best known for Lure Fishbar) as part of his Mercer Street Hospitality group, and chef John Villa, both of whom run the hotel’s anchor restaurant, Seahorse. 201 Park Avenue South, near East 17th Street Upper East Side: Nostos debuted late last March in the old Mykonian Garden space. Now under new ownership, the Greek replacement centers around braised classics, wood-fired meats, hand-crafted pies, seafood, and smoky cocktails made with hibiscus-infused mezcal. 420 East 59th Street, between Sutton Place and First Avenue Upper West Side: With just a 30-minute heads-up on Instagram, Ayat’s anticipated NYC location opened Saturday, March 28, to an almost-immediate packed house. The Palestinian chain’s new offshoot is called Ayat Hind’s Hall in honor of Hind Rajab, the five-year-old girl believed to have been killed by the Israeli Defense Forces in early 2024, notes West Side Rag. Fast-growing Ayat, which is gearing up to expand to Philly and D.C., is known for its wood-fired “pizzawarma” topped with beef shawarma and halloumi cheese. The company had declared that it wouldn’t be opening any more NYC-based locations after this one. 941 Amsterdam Avenue, at West 106th Street Upper West Side: After just a year and change, Thai fusion spot Aves rebranded under the same owners as Dong Lai Pavilion on Wednesday, March 18, with a refreshed focus on Northern Chinese cuisine. Lunch and dinner bring dim sum, Beijing duck, General Tso’s chicken, and wok-fried beef to the table seven days a week, adds West Side Rag, with regional items like typhoon shrimp, truffled steamed egg, and banana lava rolls. 102 West 86th Street, between Columbus and Amsterdam avenues West Village: Vännest, a candy store specializing in sweet-and-sour delicacies straight from Sweden, opened on Saturday, March 28, with mix-and-match bins of imported treats like Bubs, fizzy skulls, and chocolate bars. 253 Bleecker Street, near Cornelia Street Williamsburg: Balera, a new restaurant serving pizza and wine in a room featuring a disco ball, opened on March 26. Thin, Roman-style pizza is the draw here, plus smaller, snackier options like mozzarella in carrozza and Italian wines (Italy makes up 70 percent of the wine list), with many bottles under $100. It’ll bring some new late-night energy to the neighborhood, open until 1 a.m. on Fridays and Saturdays. 442 Graham Avenue, near Frost Street

article_title: The Best Brunch Spots in New York City, According to Eater Editors
author: Melissa McCart
article_summary: When it comes to brunch, a meal that can require two extra-strength Advil, the last thing anyone needs is a fight about where to eat. There are endless brunch options across the five boroughs, and while most are serving dry pancakes and $20 fried chicken sandwiches, a handful rise above the rest. Our favorite places to eat brunch in New York City serve unlimited coffee, runny eggs, breakfast sandwiches, bagels and lox, or dim sum. Newly updated to include: Andrew Carmellini’s Little Fino, Midtown Mexican Animo!, Alex Stupak’s seafood spot, the Otter, and Soho’s Bar Mercer with Preston Clark at the helm. Melissa McCart is the lead editor of the Northeast region, which covers New York City, Boston, Philadelphia, and Washington, D.C. She has over 20 years of experience in food writing and editing. She co-authored Bread and How to Eat It with baker Rick Easton for Knopf. As far as brunch, she’s more of a lowbrow bruncher. article_title: East Village Hot Spot Penny Still Favors Walk-Ins
author: Nadia Chaudhury
article_summary: Just upstairs from hit wine restaurant Claud sits Penny, the second restaurant from co-owners Chase Sinzer and chef Joshua Pinsky. It’s a tiny space that fills up fast — there are only 30 seats, and it’s mostly walk-ins. (Some reservations are available two weeks ahead of time at 9 a.m.). If you’re willing to stand, you can prop up at the standing rails along the window overlooking the sidewalk. Penny works as an elegant yet slightly kitschy seafood and wine counter. Behind the bar, look for a fish statue among the huge ice buckets and a collection of small toys. The marble bar counter is lined with adorable purple enamel lamps. The menu is all about elegant raw and cooked seafood, paired with wines from a thorough list. What to order The ice boxes are a nice way to get the full raw bar experience. We shared the basic tray ($45) for two people, including oysters, countneck clams, somehow-creamy shrimp (dip ’em into the cocktail and horseradish sauces), marinated mussels, and a black bass tartare with smoked pickled shallots and horseradish. A solo diner ordered an ice box for themselves, which is a lot of food for one person, but kudos to them. The confit oysters were surprisingly delicate. The bivalves are cooked and served in chicken fat. Build your bite with spiced Club crackers and creme fraiche, or get the fluffy sesame brioche ($8) for dipping. The rainbow trout ($48) is a delicate filet served atop beurre rouge dotted with chanterelle mushrooms. The wines Trust the staffers for recommendations. I enjoyed a mineral glass of Rioja blanco from winery Alegre Valganon. With the trout, I had a delightfully bubbly red wine, the Bukkuram sole d’agosto from Marco De Bartoli. Insider tip The Penny martini ($19) is made with barley shochu, sake, and dry vermouth. And the sole beer option is the Suntory premium malt for $12, but they also serve Cel-Ray, the celery soft drink.

article_title: Michelin Guide Teases Nine New NYC Restaurant Additions
author: Melissa McCart
article_summary: A burrito from Los Burritos Juarez. | Cole Saladino The Michelin Guide — the international dining rating system run by the French tire company — announced the latest batch of New York City restaurants to its directory today, Wednesday, April 15: They include nine Michelin-recommended restaurants with Bib Gourmand or Star potential, joining the batch announced in the fall. In this round, the number of Brooklyn nods surpasses Manhattan’s, with neighborhood places landing early recognition.  Michelin will hold a ceremony in the fall — no word yet on whereabouts or the date — during which New York restaurants and their fans will learn which ones land Stars and Bib Gourmands. They’ll find out at a fast-clip event that groups multiple Northeast cities along with Chicago. Entre Nous 39 Clifton Place, at Grand Avenue, Clinton Hill The Michelin Guide includes this 55-seat bar serving low-intervention wines for its “French-leaning small plates are cooked with intention and restraint: chicken liver parfait with cornichons, crisp mushroom croquettes with truffle aioli, and a bright Jonah crab salad that lets the seafood lead.” Entre Nous opened in 2024 as a sibling to Fradei in Fort Greene, from husband-wife team Allie Prater-Besset and Clement Besset.  Los Burritos Juárez 354 Myrtle Avenue, at Adelphi Street, Fort Greene Los Burritos Juárez has become a fast favorite for Alan Delgado’s minimalist but delicious slow-cooked stews tucked into flour tortillas in a style that comes from El Paso and Ciudad Juárez. They get the Michelin nod for “flavors are deeply comforting, offering a regional burrito tradition rarely found in New York.” Bong 724 Sterling Place, at Bedford Avenue, Crown Heights Michelin calls this Cambodian newcomer from owners Chakriya Un and Alexander Chaparro an early “neighborhood fixture” for dishes like its crisp whole fish with green mango sauce as well as “standouts like plea satch ko, a Cambodian beef salad with razor-thin meat that melts in your mouth in a delightfully funky, spicy sauce.” Vato 226 Seventh Avenue, at Third Street, Park Slope Michelin recognizes the team behind Manhattan sibling Corima (and the just-open Bar Chucho) for taking its excellent tortillas to Brooklyn at Vato, where diners should try items like “the breakfast-style burnt ends burrito filled with tender pulled meat, scrambled eggs and cheddar cheese or the verde burrito featuring braised pork shoulder tossed with salsa verde and diced potatoes.” I Cavallini 284 Grand Street, at Roebling Street, Williamsburg Sibling to the Four Horsemen, this sophomore Italian opening has dazzled diners with chef Nick Curtola’s “nervetti and onion salad, where braised beef tendons are shaved thin and paired with crisp, acidic onions; a striking dish that signals the kitchen’s comfort with depth and simplicity.” Michelin also recommends its “handmade pastas and rustic mains follow with balance and control.” MANHATTAN Le Chêne 76 Carmine Street, at Varick Street, West Village Alexia and Ronan Duchêne’s restaurant has caught Michelin’s eye with its “rich, robust cooking celebrates French excellence of yesteryear with foie gras terrines and vin jaune sauces prepared with substance and astuteness.” It calls out a bar made for walk-ins and cites the restaurant as a wine-lover’s destination. Hwaro 776 Eighth Avenue, at West 48th Street, Midtown Chef Sungchul Shim’s ambitious 22-seat counter inside steakhouse Gui gets the recognition it deserves for this standout room centered around his cooking, along with his skill in “fusing Korean flavors with French techniques” with an ambitious tasting menu. Elcielo New York Virgin Hotels, 1227 Broadway, at West 30th Street, Nomad With locations in Miami and New York, the restaurant from chef Juan Manuel Barrientos is named for his Colombian-leaning tasting menu which includes “the ‘tree of life’ bread, the chocolate experience, and an ornate coffee ceremony are signatures intertwined with courses like a shio koji-marinated duck sauced with a sabayon of honey and passion fruit.” Cove 285 West Houston Street, at Hudson Street, Hudson Square Michelin calls out chef Flynn McGarry’s most ambitious project yet for its open kitchen that’s “constantly in motion” cooking dishes “rooted in Californian sensibilities and tied to the seasons, especially as many ingredients are sourced from the team’s farm in Long Island.”

article_title: The Best New Cocktail Bars in New York City, According to Eater Editors
author: Nadia Chaudhury
article_summary: The Cherry Colored Funk cocktail at Echo Lake. | Lizzie Munro Welcome to the Eater Cocktail Heatmap, focusing on the hottest new bar openings in New York every month. Eater editors do thorough reporting on the most exciting restaurant openings to hit the city, as well as smaller openings worth having on the radar. In this monthly map, we narrow the field to cocktail bars that are drawing the most excitement, buzz, crowds, and early positive chatter, focusing on spots that have only been open for six months or less. When an Eater editor has already been to a place, we share insider tips on what to expect and what’s worth ordering as well. New to the list in April: Greenpoint neighborhood bar the Peek Inn; Lower East Side’s Cocktail Omakase; new rum destination Echo Lake in Williamsburg; the Corima team’s new downtown Mexican cocktail spot Bar Chucho; and Bodega Nights, the LES wine bar Babysips team’s venture into Bushwick. For now, we’ve removed the Argyle, 25 Hours, and Bar Maeda (which might be closed). For related drinking guides, check out Eater NY’s picks for the best cocktail bars, dive bars, and wine bars. And for more New York dining recommendations, check out the new hotspots in Manhattan, Brooklyn, and Queens. In need of an insider’s perspective on how to eat well, no matter where you are in NYC? Pick up our book: The Eater Guide to New York City.

article_title: A Six-Month-Old Omakase — And More NYC Restaurant Closures in April
author: Tierney Plumb
article_summary: A piece at Sushidokoro Mekumi. | Sushidokoro Mekumi This is Eater’s guide to all the New York City restaurants, bars, and cafes that closed in April 2026. This list will be updated weekly (see: March, February, January), serving as a round-up of the dining and drinking places that have shuttered around the city. If a restaurant or bar has closed in your neighborhood, let us know at ny@eater.com. April 10 Spotlight Hudson Square: Sushidokoro Mekumi, an ambitious, eight-seat omakase counter specializing in seafood from Japan’s Ishikawa Prefecture, suddenly went dark after just six months. Blogger the Sushi Legend confirmed the closure on Wednesday, April 8, reposting the restaurant’s response to reservation requests: “Due to unforeseen circumstances, we are currently unable to continue operations.” Its website is now inactive. Chef Hajime Kumabe, a protégé of Takayoshi Yamaguchi, the esteemed master sushi chef behind the decades-old original in Japan, led the Japanese import with two nightly seatings. Mekumi’s tiny, windowless NYC offshoot maintained its focus on seasonal fish caught off the western coast in the Sea of Japan, presented in a progression of 18 courses for $300. The remote region is known for delicacies like snow crab, which was flown in weekly during its short winter season to star in a special tasting menu priced at $888 (!) per person. Sake sourced from Japan’s premier Kokuryu Brewery was also showcased on-site. It’s unclear if its recently opened and on-site Bar Maeda, led by Tokyo-based Mori Bar’s Yoshikatsu Maeda, is still open — the current number rings out. 70 Charlton Street, between Hudson and Varick streets And the rest… East Village: NYC’s fast-growing Cantonese chain Dim Sum Palace will close its East Village location on Sunday, April 12, per EV Grieve, flagging signage on the door thanking customers for their “support, trust and kindness” over its nearly eight-year run. The decade-old original in Times Square, as well as four others across Manhattan, remain open. 59 Second Avenue, between Third and Fourth streets Greenpoint: Brooklyn Brine, a popular pickle purveyor since 2010, closed its only physical address after just a year. Owner and head briner Shamus Jones says he was given short notice to vacate his home in the back of the 42 West building on Tuesday, April 7, pointing to “stupid Nepo-boomers that vote for Trump, who exercise control over small friends and family investment,” as the alleged cause. The walk-in storefront doubled as a warehouse to bottle his kimchi half-sours, preserved lemons, spicy bloody mary pickles, and other seasonal creations. He started the brand in the kitchen of Greenpoint’s now-closed restaurant Brooklyn Label, going on to pickle around the borough before coming back to the neighborhood where it all started last year. While he’ll still supply the NYC region with “awesome shit,” including some new pickles, it won’t be under the Brooklyn Brine moniker. He’s moving back to the Pacific Northwest. 42 West Street, Unit 112, at Noble Street April 3 Spotlight Hell’s Kitchen: It’s the end of an era for the Meatball Shop, the once-skyrocketing NYC chain that closed its last-remaining storefront in late March. The bro-dude magnet for mix-and-match meatball dishes — with a cringe-y “show us your balls” tag line — got its start in 2010 on the Lower East Side, quickly ballooning to seven fast-casual locations across Manhattan and Brooklyn (plus a short-lived attempt in D.C.) at its peak, in addition to having a cookbook and red-sauce line in grocery aisles. The pandemic and lease issues whittled down its portfolio, and as of 2023, Hell’s Kitchen was the only location left. “After many great years, The Meatball Shop has closed its doors,” per a statement on its website. Its long-gone founders, Daniel Holzman and Michael Chernow, are now in LA (Danny Boy’s Famous Original Pizza) and the podcast/wellness world, respectively. Back in December, however, the childhood friends teased a Meatball Shop reboot, polling Instagram followers on a “crazy, but potentially genius idea” to take back and restore the brand to its “original glory” in 2026. 798 Ninth Avenue, at West 53rd Street And the rest… Bowery: After a valiant 10-year run, East Village watering hole the Gray Mare was put out to pasture the last weekend of March. The team teases opening something new, though: “As we look ahead, we are beyond excited to begin a new chapter right here with a new concept in the very near future,” per a closing statement on Instagram, coming in the summer. Followers flooded the comments with Gray Mare memories, from engagements to open-mic nights at the pub known for its lengthy happy hour, Cobb salad, fish and chips, and unique beers on tap. 61 Second Avenue, near East 4th Street Gowanus: Strong Rope, the Brooklyn brewery that relies on all-NY hops and malt to make 60 beers like its flagship Wolf in the Garden IPA, closed the decade-old Gowanus location that started it all at the end of March. Per Brooklyn Paper, fledgling beermaker Forever Homebrewing plans to take over the space and change its name to Forever Brewing. Strong Hope’s husband-wife owners, Christina Quintero and Jason Sahler are now going all-in on its waterfront hub in Red Hook, home to its five-year-old taproom, consolidated production facilities, an event space, and even a bookstore. 574 President Street, near Fourth Avenue Hell’s Kitchen: Sergimmo Salumeria — the go-to Italian trattoria and market near the Javits Center since 2010 — quietly closed right after Saint Patrick’s Day, per W42ndSt. The old-school staple lured locals and tourists alike with its house-made mozzarella, hearty Italian sandwiches, and minestrone advertised on a sandwich board out front and dry-aged meats hanging in the window. 9th Avenue, between West 35th and 36th Street  Park Slope: The V Spot, a vegan restaurant specializing in Latin street snacks like empanadas and chicharrones for 20 years, closed its sole Brooklyn location, according to Park Slope News. Founded by two Colombian American brothers, the V Spot featured a walk-up ordering counter and a backyard patio for enjoying organic wines. Per its website, the V Spot’s empanadas found a new home in the East Village as a weekend pop-up inside St. Marks Comedy Club. 156 Fifth Avenue between Douglass and Degraw streets Park Slope: Yamato, the casual Japanese staple for reliable sushi, sashimi, and noodles for the past 30 years, closed at the end of March. Local blog Here’s Park Slope spoke to a manager, who cited that there were not enough sales, the lease was expiring, and the landlord’s uptick in rent, leaving the owner no choice but to close; Instagram commenters tagged NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani to stop the small business bleedout. 168 Seventh Avenue, near First Street Tribeca: Elegant Tuscan restaurant Il Giglio, the Lower Manhattan offshoot of downtown stalwart Il Mulino since 2018, served its last plate of cacio e pepe on Saturday, March 28. “Our Tribeca dining room has closed as we transition to a new [TBA] location,” per a statement on its website on Thursday, April 2, with reopening details coming “soon.” The mod dining room, with white-washed brick walls, a matte-black ceiling, and hand-blown glass orbs, switched its name from Il Mulino to Il Giglio in 2023 under longtime partner Jerry Katzoff. An alleged eviction dispute in court sparked the closure at the current address, a former manager told Eater via email. The original Il Mulino, situated at the foot of Washington Square Park since 1981, remains open as a separate entity. 361 Greenwich Street, near Harrison Street

article_title: The ‘Top Chef’ Winner Who Wants to Open a Latin Halal Bodega
author: Nadia Chaudhury
article_summary: Danny Garcia wearing Only Goods apparel. | Sebastian Mejias Danny Garcia, the winner of Top Chef in season 21, came off that win on a high in 2024, ahead of opening a new restaurant under restaurateur James Kent’s group, in the Flatiron, Time and Tide. But coinciding with his win, Kent died suddenly, and everything changed. Just a year later, with shakeups and rebranding of the company, and a concept that didn’t quite land, Garcia’s restaurant closed.  Originally, the idea was that Garcia and Kent Hospitality Group would revamp the Time and Tide space into something new while it was being used as a temporary pop-up for Italian restaurant Massara. But since then, Garcia has regrouped, stepping away from the company and starting anew with his own line of products — jarred salsa macha and T-shirts — along with plans to open his own restaurant, under the name Only Goods.   “Only Goods was born of this idea of putting a spotlight on Latin American culture, but also a nod to younger Danny Garcia and the dream I had to open a restaurant,” he tells me. Garcia wants Only Goods to work somewhere between the ubiquitous Puerto Rican bodegas in NYC, “small spaces with dusty cans of beans and there’s one of everything across the wooden shelf,” he says, and the overabundance within Cracker Barrels. For the eventual daytime restaurant, he wants it to work as what he describes as a luncheonette and a bodega counter up front, and also a casual and cozy restaurant in the back. “Community-first” is a value Garcia mentions often. “We are going to be a restaurant that embodies the neighborhood and what the people need it to be, like a bodega.” “Time and Tide closing was hard,” Garcia says. “I put everything into that restaurant, so taking that to the chin. It’s not a failure; it was an accomplishment. Did it not last as long as I would have wanted? Sure, but there are learned lessons, and now I get to take those lessons and do the next best thing.” At the time, Garcia, who has a Puerto Rican and Dominican background, had to figure out what he’d do next, often talking it out with his wife, pastry chef Sumaiya Bangee. “I need to do something that is just wholeheartedly Danny Garcia,” he recounts thinking. And he remembered his wish as a kid coming up in restaurant kitchens of running a sandwich shop, leading to his Only Goods plans. Garcia knows that Puerto Rican food is usually seen as more fast-casual, where “aunties and titís and abuelas cook from steam tables, or you’re getting food on paper plates,” he says. “Let’s shine the light on that, take up our own space, and let’s be the catalyst to open this cuisine a little more and say, ‘Yeah, we can do more than rice and beans and pernil.’” He wants to do for Puerto Rican and Latin American cuisines what chefs are doing for Indian and Afro-Caribbean cuisines in New York now.  There’s also a notable restriction for Only Goods: Garcia is Muslim, so he won’t serve pork, which might be unexpected for a Puerto Rican restaurant. He knows there will be questions about where lechon and pernil are. “Pork’s off the table,” he says, “so what other delicious succulent things can we do?” Garcia sees the restaurant being halal as another extension of himself and being for his community. “I want Muslims to be able to roll through and be like, ‘I can eat everything on the menu.” Before Garcia opens the restaurant — there is no lease or address yet, he’s been searching — he’s launching Only Goods as a brand with streetwear and merchandising. (More and more, food brands are getting into clothing and wearable goods, like Barney Greengrass and Cup Noodles.) “It’s very fun, laidback, not stuffy,” Garcia says. He tagged Brooklyn-based Nash Studios, led by Alexander King, whom he met through Upper West Side Chino Latino restaurant La Dinastia, and they just vibed. It’s apt that Only Goods abbreviates to OG — “it’s the original, it’s the highest level,” Garcia says. Expect hats, shirts, sweatshirts, tote bags, matchbooks, ashtrays, long candles, and more.  And the brand goes hand in hand with Garcia’s food and cooking. Only Goods launched with the salsa macha, which’ll eventually be followed with a guava-tamarind barbecue glaze that he describes as “tangy and spicy.” He’s made a barbecue lamb rib using it.  In fact, he’s cooked those lamb ribs at pop-ups like Theodora last December and plans to do so for the upcoming Pebble Beach Food & Wine this week. For Eid, he debuted Only Goods at his co-hosted dinner at Platform by JBF with chef and cookbook author Ifrah F. Ahmed, where they cooked a Somali-Latin dinner. Next up, he’s got a collaboration at Nashville restaurant Rolf & Daughters on Sunday, April 19.  However the restaurant shakes out, Garcia wants to make sure it’s fun and reflective of who he is. He doesn’t want to cook and feel like he needs to create Spanish food with French techniques, he says. “It’s boring, we’ve done it, we’ve all tasted it, we’ve all seen it. Let’s be a little original and not be afraid to just be different and just be us unapologetically.”

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Queens, According to Eater Editors
author: Caroline Shin
article_summary: Mohinga at Sophie's. | Caroline Shin Welcome to the Eater Heatmap, focusing on the hottest new openings in Queens this month. Eater editors do thorough reporting on the most exciting restaurant openings to hit their city, as well as smaller debuts on the radar. In this map, we narrow the field to those places in Queens that are drawing the most excitement, buzz, and crowds, focusing largely on restaurants that have only been open for six months or less. When an Eater editor has already been to a place — even if it just opened — we share insider tips on what to expect and what’s worth ordering, as well. New to the list in April: Nigerian restaurant Wakky’s D in Jamaica and Sophie’s Rangoon Kitchen in Elmhurst. For more New York dining recommendations, check out the new hotspots in Brooklyn and Manhattan. And for an insider’s perspective on how to eat well no matter where you are in NYC, pick up our book: The Eater Guide to New York City.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Brooklyn, According to Eater Editors
author: Nadia Chaudhury
article_summary: A dish at Pies ‘n’ Thighs. | Paul Quitoriano Welcome to the Eater Heatmap, focusing on the hottest new openings in Brooklyn this month. Eater editors do thorough reporting on the most exciting restaurant openings to hit their city, as well as smaller openings worth having on the radar. In this map, we narrow the field to those places in Brooklyn that are drawing the most excitement, buzz, crowds, and early positive chatter, focusing largely on restaurants that have only been open for six months or less. When an Eater editor has already been to a place, we share insider tips on what to expect and what’s worth ordering as well. New to the list in April: Williamsburg pizza and Italian restaurant Balera; the Prospect Heights expansion of fried chicken joint Pies ‘n’ Thighs; and the reopening of Northeast-focused tasting menu Honey Badger. And, for now, we’ve removed Falansai, Barker Cafeteria, Third Time’s the Charm, Il Leone, and Vato. For more New York dining recommendations, check out the new hotspots in Manhattan and Queens. And for an insider’s perspective on how to eat well no matter where you are in NYC, pick up our book: The Eater Guide to New York City.
